_clinical_notes: Key differentials: (1) AC joint sprain — direct blow to shoulder point, step deformity = Grade III+ seek care. (2) Rotator cuff tear — sudden pop with arm raise, weakness > pain, inability to raise arm = seek care. (3) Shoulder dislocation — arm held externally rotated/abducted, obvious deformity = emergency seek care. (4) Impingement — gradual overhead pain, painful arc 60-120°, no acute trauma = conservative. (5) FOOSH — clavicle bony tenderness = possible fracture. Grades: I = micro-tear/sprain, conservative. II = partial tear, conservative + monitor. III = complete disruption, often needs eval.

mechanism.prompt: How did the shoulder get injured?

options.label: Direct blow or fall on the shoulder point
Fell on an outstretched hand (FOOSH)
Arm was pulled or yanked
Gradual pain from overhead activity / throwing

deformity_check.prompt: Compare both shoulders. Is there a visible bump or step-off deformity at the top of the injured shoulder?

options.label: Yes, visible step or bump at the shoulder tip
No visible deformity — just pain

clavicle_check.prompt: Press along the collarbone from your neck to your shoulder. Is there sharp point tenderness anywhere along the bone?

options.label: Yes, one specific spot is very tender
General soreness but no specific spot

dislocation_check.prompt: Does the shoulder look like it's out of place, or is the arm held in an unusual position away from the body?

options.label: Yes, looks clearly out of position
No, position looks normal
Gradual over days or weeks
Sudden — something snapped or gave out during activity

pop_shoulder.prompt: Did you feel a pop, and can you still raise your arm overhead?

options.label: Pop + can still raise arm (painful)
Pop + cannot raise arm / extreme weakness

arc_pain.prompt: Where in the range of motion is the pain worst?

options.label: When raising arm between shoulder height and overhead
At the end range only (fully overhead)
Throughout all movement

arm_raise.prompt: Can you raise the injured arm out to the side and overhead?

options.label: Yes, full range with some pain
Limited — can't get past shoulder height
Cannot raise arm at all / severe weakness

grade_shoulder.prompt: How would you describe the pain level?

options.label: Mild — noticeable but not limiting daily activity
Moderate — limiting overhead activities

outcome.seek_care_reason: A visible step deformity at the shoulder tip indicates a significant AC joint separation (Grade III or higher). This needs imaging and evaluation.
Point tenderness along the collarbone after a fall may indicate a clavicle fracture. You should get an X-ray.
A shoulder dislocation needs to be reduced (put back in place) by a medical professional. Do not attempt to put it back yourself.
Inability to raise the arm or sudden complete weakness after a pop may indicate a rotator cuff tear. You need an evaluation and possibly an MRI.

outcome.monitor_note: If no improvement in 7 days or weakness develops, seek evaluation.
